description:
You are a performance engineer who has just finished collecting raw profiling logs for a micro-service.  
    The logs were automatically deposited for you in  

        /home/user/perf_logs/  

    Each log file has the extension “.log”.  
    You now need to create a compressed backup so that the raw logs can be moved off the machine.

    Your goal
    =========
    1. Create a gzip-compressed tar archive that contains **all** “.log” files that are currently inside  
       /home/user/perf_logs/ (there are no sub-directories).  
       • The archive **must** be named:  
         perf_logs_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
         where YYYYMMDD is today’s calendar date in the machine’s local time zone.  
       • The archive file must end up in the directory:  
         /home/user/backups/  

    2. For auditing purposes, after you have created the archive, write a human-readable report file called  
       backup_report.txt  in the same /home/user/backups/ directory.  
       The report must be plain ASCII text and follow **exactly** the format shown below:

            Archive Name : <archive_file_name>
            Creation UTC : <yyyy-mm-ddThh:mm:ssZ>
            Included Logs:
            <log_file_1>  <size_in_bytes>
            <log_file_2>  <size_in_bytes>
            ...
            TOTAL FILES  : <number_of_files>
            TOTAL BYTES  : <sum_of_bytes>

       Formatting requirements:
       • Each key (Archive Name, Creation UTC, etc.) starts at column 1.  
       • Values are single-space separated from their keys.  
       • The “Creation UTC” value is an ISO-8601 timestamp in **UTC** with trailing “Z”.  
       • The “Included Logs” section lists one log file per line, each followed by two consecutive spaces
         and then the exact filesize in bytes (use POSIX stat size, no commas or units).  
       • There must be no blank lines anywhere in the report.

    3. Verify your work by listing the backup directory contents and displaying the report to the terminal.

    Hints (do NOT output these literally):
    • You will probably need to make the /home/user/backups directory first.  
    • Standard GNU tar/gzip tools are available.  
    • date, stat, and wc might help obtain date strings, timestamps, and sizes.

    When you finish, the automated grader will check:
    • that /home/user/backups/perf_logs_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip-compressed tar file;  
    • that the tarball contains only the original “.log” files and nothing else;  
    • that backup_report.txt exists and matches the exact format & content specification;  
    • that the sizes and counts in the report are accurate.

    You do NOT need root privileges to complete this task.
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent begins:

    1. Directory /home/user/perf_logs/ already exists and contains exactly three files:

       /home/user/perf_logs/cpu_profile.log
       /home/user/perf_logs/memory_profile.log
       /home/user/perf_logs/io_profile.log

       Their initial contents (small, plain text) are:

       cpu_profile.log
       ---------------
       time,usage
       0,12
       1,18
       2,17

       memory_profile.log
       ------------------
       time,usage
       0,153600
       1,158720
       2,162304

       io_profile.log
       --------------
       time,bytes
       0,4096
       1,8192
       2,6144

    2. None of those files change after the agent starts.

    Expected end state AFTER agent completes correctly (assuming the task is run on 2025-04-09; the grader will substitute whatever today’s date is):

    1. A new directory /home/user/backups/ exists (mode 755 or similar accessible by the user).

    2. Inside /home/user/backups/ there are exactly two files:

       /home/user/backups/perf_logs_20250409.tar.gz
       /home/user/backups/backup_report.txt

    3. The tar.gz archive, when decompressed, yields exactly the three .log files listed above with identical content & timestamps preserved (mtime can vary, but inode data and byte-for-byte content must match).

    4. backup_report.txt exact expected content (timestamps & byte counts shown here for illustration; the grader calculates them dynamically):

       Archive Name : perf_logs_20250409.tar.gz
       Creation UTC : 2025-04-09T14:23:35Z
       Included Logs:
       cpu_profile.log  48
       memory_profile.log  63
       io_profile.log  54
       TOTAL FILES  : 3
       TOTAL BYTES  : 165

       Note: The byte counts above correspond to the exact character counts of the provided files including newlines.

    5. No extra files are present in /home/user/backups/ or /home/user/perf_logs/.

    Validation actions performed by the grader:

       • tar -tzf to list archive contents and confirm only the three .log filenames.  
       • sha256sum comparison between archived files and originals.  
       • Pattern match on backup_report.txt using a strict regular expression ensuring formatting and numeric correctness.  
       • Cross-check of TOTAL FILES and TOTAL BYTES against actual counts/sums.
